text:
RICE is quietly breaking.

Not because the math is wrong. Because the denominator collapsed.

When AI coding tools cut build effort by 3-5x, every feature suddenly scores "high ROI." Your backlog stops sorting itself. Everything looks worth doing.

And yet the oldest stat in product still holds: most features shipped in B2B SaaS go rarely or never used. That number hasn't budged in a decade of "better" frameworks. Which tells you the bottleneck was never build capacity. It was judgment about what to build.

The frameworks getting real traction now — Opportunity Solution Trees, JTBD bets, outcome contracts — all share one move. They stop scoring on cost and start scoring on outcome confidence.

The question shifts from "what can we afford to build?" to "what outcome are we willing to own?"

That's a much harder conversation. It forces a team to commit to a number a customer or the business will actually feel. You can't hide behind a velocity chart.

If engineering capacity isn't your scarce resource anymore, what is — for your team specifically?

#ProductManagement #Prioritization #ProductStrategy

diagram.title: THE OUTCOME OWNERSHIP STACK
diagram.subtitle: How prioritization shifts when build cost stops being the constraint

steps.label: NAME THE OUTCOME
steps.description: Pick a metric a customer or the business will actually feel.

steps.label: RATE YOUR CONFIDENCE
steps.description: How sure are you this bet moves that number?

steps.label: DEFINE THE EVIDENCE
steps.description: What signal proves or kills the bet early?

steps.label: COMMIT TO OWNERSHIP
steps.description: One team owns the outcome, not just the ship date.

steps.label: KILL OR DOUBLE DOWN
steps.description: Review against the metric, not the roadmap.
